metadata.title: Bringing Colors to Life: How We Built an AI Colors Song for Toddlers

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Three-to-five-year-old brains learn through rhythm and rhyme, so the song was built on that science. What set the project apart was more than pedagogy; the production choices mattered too. Static subtitles don't serve early learners, so the visuals had to move with the words. Colors is an upbeat, lyric-driven song designed deliberately around how young brains actually acquire meaning.

artifactLines: Three-to-five-year-old brains learn through rhythm and rhyme, so the song was built on that science.
What set the project apart was more than pedagogy; the production choices mattered too.
Static subtitles don't serve early learners, so the visuals had to move with the words.
Colors is an upbeat, lyric-driven song designed deliberately around how young brains actually acquire meaning.

beats.narration_text: The claim that three-to-five-year-old brains learn through rhythm and rhyme, and that static subtitles fail early learners, made me want to see the design choices up close. Help me: (1) break the 'Colors' song into the specific cognitive hooks, beat, repetition, rhyme, that aid a toddler; (2) compare static versus animated subtitles for a pre-reader and say why one wins; (3) draft a second micro-song for a different concept using the exact same pedagogy-first blueprint.
